content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 10, 2018

Angelina Cacioppo Hernandez
Community Engagement Coordinator
Sonoma County Library
211 E Street
Santa Rosa, CA 95404

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-17-285

Dear Ms. Cacioppo Hernandez:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Sonoma County Library regarding the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").¹

     Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

     Can the Library facilitate the means for the public to communicate with elected officials via postcard without registering as a lobbyist, lobbying firm, lobbyist employer, lobbying coalition, or $5,000 filer?

CONCLUSION

     Yes. The facts presented do not indicate that the Library is making payments for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action.

FACTS

     The Sonoma County Library has a new program, funded by a federal grant under the Library Services and Technology Act and monitored by the California State Library, entitled Together at the Table. Together at the Table encourages and facilitates discussion around current issues and topics in the news. One aspect of this program involves the Library providing postcards in its branches for the public to write their opinions and thoughts on any of the topics discussed.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-285
Page No. 2

These postcards are provided by the Library's funds, and Library staff mails the postcards on behalf
of the members of the public. The postcard writer can choose to send the postcard to a qualifying
official, such as an elected official, or any other individual of their choice.

Library management's intention behind the program is to provide an avenue for the public
to share their voice and remove any barriers in doing so. The Library is not registered as a lobbyist,
lobbying firm, lobbyist employer, lobbying coalition, or $5,000 filer, and management does not
intend for the postcards to be used as a means of lobbying a specific issue or policy matter.

ANALYSIS

Sections 86100 through 86300 of the Act establish registration requirements for individual
lobbyists, lobbying firms, and lobbyist employers and reporting requirements for individual
lobbyists, lobbying firms, lobbyist employers, and persons who directly or indirectly make payments
to influence legislative or administrative action of $5,000 or more in any calendar quarter (a
"$5,000 filer").²

Under Section 86115(b), any "persons" who directly or indirectly makes payments of
$5,000 or more in a calendar quarter to influence state legislative or administrative action must file
quarterly reports like those required of lobbyist employers.

Section 82045 defines "payments to influence legislative or administrative action." Under
subdivision (e), the only pertinent part, a payment to influence legislative or administrative action
includes a "payment for or in connection with soliciting or urging other persons to enter into direct
communication with any elective state official, legislative official or agency official."

You have stated that the Library strives to "encourage the community,… to voice [its]
thoughts and opinions on important topics with decision-makers" in funding, providing, and
mailing postcards on behalf of the public.

In the Hinman Advice Letter, we advised that an organization, which provided sample
letters for its members to sign and send to legislators and the Governor, had to report its cost
associated with this activity as a payment to influence legislative or administrative action because
the organization's "primary purpose" was "to advocate support or opposition to legislative or
administrative action." (Hinman Advice Letter, No. A-00-281.)

This case is distinguishable from the one in Hinman because here the Library provides blank
postcards to members of the public and does not draft sample language or attempt to influence
members of the public through their content. The Library does not suggest that Library patrons
support or oppose any particular legislative or administrative action through the use of these
postcards. Patrons are free to write anything they wish and are free to send the postcards to
whomever they prefer. The Library's principal purpose in funding, supplying, and mailing the
postcards is neither to support, promote, influence, modify, oppose, delay, nor advance any
legislative or administrative action.

² Based on the facts provided, the Library is not a lobbyist, lobbying firm, or lobbyist employer because the
Library does not meet the definitions found in Sections 82039, 82038.5, or 82039.5, respectively.

File No. A-17-285
Page No. 3

Therefore, these actions do not constitute "payment for or in connection with soliciting or
urging other persons to enter into direct communication with any elective state official, legislative
official, or agency official." Thus, the Library is not making payments for the purpose of
influencing legislative or administrative action and is not required to register as a lobbyist, $5,000
filer, or any other lobbying entity.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

Ryan P. O'Connor
By: Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:jgl

sections.analysis:
Sections 86100 through 86300 of the Act establish registration requirements for individual 
lobbyists, lobbying firms, and lobbyist employers and reporting requirements for individual 
lobbyists, lobbyist firms, lobbyist employers, and persons who directly or indirectly make payments 
to influence legislative or administrative action of $5,000 or more in any calendar quarter (a 
"$5,000 filer"). 2 
Under Section 8611 S(b ), any "persons" who directly or indirectly makes payments of 
$5,000 or more in a calendar quarter to influence state legislative or administrative action must file 
quarterly reports like those required oflobbyist employers. 
Section 82045 defines "payments to influence legislative or administrative action." Under 
subdivision (e), the only pertinent part, a payment to influence legislative or administrative action 
includes a "payment for or in connection with soliciting or urging other persons to enter into direct 
communication with any elective state official, legislative official or agency official." 
You have stated that the Library strives to "encourage the community ... to voice [its] 
thoughts and opinions on important topics with decision-makers" in funding, providing, and 
mailing postcards on behalf of the public. 
In the Hinman Advice Letter, we advised that an organization, which provided sample 
letters for its members to sign and send to legislators and the Governor, had to report its cost 
associated with this activity as a payment to influence legislative or administrative action because 
the organization's "primary purpose" was "to advocate support or opposition to legislative or 
administrative action." (Hinman Advice Letter, No. A-00-281.) 
This case is distinguishable from the one in Hinman because here the Library provides blank

whomever they prefer. The Library's principal purpose in funding, supplying, and mailing the 
postcards is neither to support, promote, influence, modify, oppose, delay, nor advance any 
legislative or administrative action. 
2 Based on the facts provided, the Library is not a lobbyist, lobbying firm, or lobbyist employer because the 
Library does not meet the definitions found in Sections 82039, 82038.5, or 82039.5, respectively.  
Therefore, these actions do not constitute "payment for or in connection with soliciting or 
urging other persons to enter into direct communication with any elective state official, legislative 
official, or agency official." Thus, the Library is not making payments for the purpose of 
influencing legislative or administrative action and is not required to register as a lobbyist, $5,000 
filer, or any other lobbying entity.

QUESTION: Can the Library facilitate the means for the public to communicate with elected officials via 
postcard without registering as a lobbyist, lobbying firm, lobbyist employer, lobbying coalition, or 
$5,000 filer?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The facts presented do not indicate that the Library is making payments for the purpose 
of influencing any legislative or administrative action.
